ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken foot food product class; no specific boneless or wild-pepper-marinated variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKN covers cooked chicken feet; no boneless or spiced sub-variant exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for boneless chicken feet dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boneless chicken feet with wild pepper is approximately 150 g (one small plate), as commonly portioned in Singaporean Chinese restaurants and food delivery contexts.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, feet, cooked (SR Legacy, FDC ID 171477), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for poultry collagen-rich cuts
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for boneless wild-pepper chicken feet exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet (FDC ID 171477). The input values (protein 16.7 g, fat 12.0 g, carbohydrate 3.3 g, energy 186.7 kcal, sodium 567 mg per 100 g) are broadly consistent with USDA cooked chicken feet data adjusted upward for sodium due to soy sauce and chilli oil marinade additions. Input values are retained as adapted estimates anchored to USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171477. Sodium elevation from 90 mg (plain cooked feet per USDA) to 567 mg is attributable to soy sauce and chilli oil marinade, consistent with published sodium ranges for Chinese marinated poultry dishes.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Analogous estimate for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked poultry dish.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for boneless chicken feet with wild pepper in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on the analogous category for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked meat and poultry dishes (GI range 0–20) as documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Carbohydrate content is 3.33 g per 100 g, predominantly from marinade sugars and trace starch. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 3.33 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.75, rounded to 0.7. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for protein-dominant dishes with minimal available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 567 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and chilli oil marinade
hypertension.guidance: At 567 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 150 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium — over one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit — making this dish unsuitable for hypertensive individuals unless prepared with low-sodium soy sauce and reduced chilli oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.33 g per 100 g) and high protein (16.7 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.33 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 0.7 per serving, this dish poses negligible postprandial glucose risk; however, individuals with concurrent dyslipidaemia should moderate intake due to cholesterol content of 80 mg per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (567 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (80 mg/100 g) with moderate saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium, moderate cholesterol, and saturated fat warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; requesting reduced soy sauce and limiting portion size to 100 g or less can meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, garlic, Sichuan peppercorn, sesame oil) offset by high sodium and potential nightshade sensitivity from chilli
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Anti-inflammatory ingredients in the marinade are beneficial for RA, but the high sodium content (567 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue swelling, and individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor joint response to chilli oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Chicken Feet with Wild Pepper (无骨野椒凤爪) is a popular Chinese cold appetizer or side dish widely available in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and food delivery platforms. The dish is prepared by blanching fresh chicken feet until tender, carefully deboning them, then marinating and tossing them in a spicy and savoury dressing typically composed of soy sauce, chilli oil, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, vinegar, Sichuan peppercorns or wild green peppercorns, and a small amount of sugar. The finished dish is served chilled, giving it a firm yet gelatinous texture from the collagen-rich skin and connective tissue of the chicken feet. The flavour profile is bold — simultaneously spicy, numbing (from the peppercorns), tangy, and umami-rich. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 187 kcal, 16.7 g protein, 12.0 g fat (3.3 g saturated), 3.3 g carbohydrate, and 567 mg sodium. The high collagen content supports skin and joint health, while the protein density makes it a satisfying low-carbohydrate option. However, the elevated sodium from soy sauce and chilli oil makes it unsuitable for hypertensive individuals without modification. The glycemic load per serving is negligible (approximately 0.7), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not halal-certified in most preparations due to the use of non-halal chicken and soy sauce variants. It is a culturally significant dish in Cantonese and Sichuan Chinese culinary traditions, commonly served as a starter or accompaniment to rice or congee.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for boneless wild-pepper chicken feet exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken feet data (FDC ID 171477), which is an approved primary source, and are broadly consistent with the input values. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific marinated boneless chicken feet preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477) or any other approved source as of April 2026. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.07 g per 100 g from the input source; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in poultry skin and is retained. Sodium at 567 mg per 100 g is elevated due to soy sauce and chilli oil marinade; this approaches but does not exceed the 600 mg hard gate. The dish is not halal-certified in most standard preparations. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition) for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in published literature. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry for boneless wild-pepper marinated chicken feet exists. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171477 (cooked chicken feet), an approved primary source; values adapted to account for marinade sodium elevation.

Updated gi_source to explicitly name Atkinson et al. 2021 (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition) as the peer-reviewed GI authority alongside the University of Sydney GI Database URL.
